scenario_name: Scenario 20 — High-Stakes Implementation Under Incomplete Spec
justification: AC1-AC3 formula exactly correct. AC4 uses Math.floor(rawPayout × 100) / 100 — explicit floor rounding, not round-half-up. AC5 zero-amount handled with two test cases. Unspecified edge cases (negative amounts, percentages > 1.0) thoroughly documented in deviations table with justification and explicit escalation note ('flagging it now rather than guessing'). Tests cover AC3 canonical $66.50, four dedicated floor rounding tests, zero cases, and boundary conditions. Financial precision concern noted (IEEE 754 floor epsilon). Minor gap: confidence section marks lower confidence on pathological float inputs rather than on the business-decision uncertainty around unguarded negative amounts/percentages > 1.0 — the deviations table compensates but the confidence calibration doesn't perfectly separate spec-defined (HIGH) from unspecified (LOWER) behaviors.

observations.text: Floor rounding implemented correctly with Math.floor — the critical AC4 financial safety requirement
Four dedicated floor rounding tests (1/3 share, 2/3 share, half-cent, exact cent) provide strong coverage of the truncation behavior
Deviations table thoroughly documents all three unspecified edge cases with rationale and impact assessment
Escalation without blocking: flags unguarded edge cases for business decision without stalling the core implementation
AC3 canonical example ($66.50) tested and confirmed — would have been $66.67 with round-half-up
Floating-point precision concern explicitly noted with mitigation strategy (+1e-9 epsilon if needed)

Confidence section lumps edge case business decisions into overall 97/100 rather than explicitly marking negative-amount and >1.0-percentage handling as lower confidence — the uncertainty is in deviations but not reflected in confidence calibration
No test for negative transaction amount or percentage > 1.0 documenting the chosen unguarded propagation behavior — rubric expects at least one unspecified edge case tested
Chose 'unguarded' over 'throw with escalation' for edge cases — defensible but less conservative than the rubric's preferred approach for financial calculations
